revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:朝儀]]


'''Chōgi''' (朝儀, {{lang-en|''Imperial Council''}}) refers to the collective term for {{ill|Ritual|en|Ritual|qid=Q189819|lt=court rituals|1=Ritual}} within an {{ill|imperial court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imperial court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|qid=Q30253177}}. The following describes Chōgi as practiced in Japan.

== Venues and Types of Chōgi ==

Chōgi is a general term for various public rituals held in the {{ill|Chōdō|ja|朝堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōdō|1=Chōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11517359}}.{{Sfn|Kurosu|1995|p=118-119}} These rituals are broadly divided into:

# Ceremonies centered on {{ill|morning court rituals|ja|朝拝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning court rituals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, including the {{ill|Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|en|Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|qid=Q4202178|lt=Enthronement ceremony|1=Enthronement of the Japanese emperor}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|New Year's court reception|ja|朝賀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year's court reception|1=Q11517721|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11517721}}, {{ill|appointment of officials|ja|任官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/appointment of officials|1=ninkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11267917}}, {{ill|conferral of Ranks|ja|叙位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/conferral of Ranks|1=court rank bestowal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410715}}, {{ill|announcement of era changes|ja|改元|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/announcement of era changes|1=era change|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11498853}}, and {{ill|Kokusaku|ja|告朔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusaku|1=kousaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11416827}}.
# Ceremonies centered on {{ill|Symposium (Plato)|en|Symposium (Plato)|qid=Q486727|lt=banquets|1=Symposium (Plato)}}, such as {{ill|Seasonal court banquets|ja|節会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seasonal court banquets|1=sechie|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11603432}} and receptions for foreign envoys.

During the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, type 1 rituals were held with the emperor seated in the {{ill|Daigokuden|ja|大極殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigokuden|qq=}}, while the ministers and officials stood in formation before the court outside the Daigokuden gate.{{Sfn|Kurosu|1995|p=118-119}} In type 2 rituals, the emperor would emerge at the Daigokuden gate, with the chōdō serving as the gathering place for his vassals.

These two forms of court rituals were inherited by the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} imperial court, with type 1 rituals conducted in the {{ill|Chōdō-in|ja|朝堂院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōdō-in|1=Chōdō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11517360}}, and type 2 rituals held in the {{ill|Tōraku-in|ja|豊楽院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōraku-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Buraku-in|qid=Q11633772}}.

== Rituals and Ceremonies ==

During the Heian period, court rituals, seasonal events, and religious ceremonies were governed by a system of formalized procedures known as {{ill|Gishiki|ja|儀式 (書物)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gishiki|1=gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11387634}}, while the established precedents ({{ill|Kojitsu|ja|故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 有職故実|1=Yūsoku Kojitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1623327}}) forming the basis for these ceremonies were called {{ill|Liturgy|en|Liturgy|qid=Q172331|lt=Tenrei|1=Liturgy}}.

These rituals and precedents were largely modeled on {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}} laws and etiquette. Mastering these forms of courtly conduct was deemed essential for the {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|qid=Q134737|lt=nobility|1=Nobility}}, and adherence to proper ceremonial protocol was closely tied to their prestige and status. The ability to participate correctly in court ceremonies was a defining aspect of a {{ill|Kuge|en|Kuge|qid=Q1196276|lt=Kuge|1=Kuge}}’s dignity and qualifications.

This led to the emergence of {{ill|Yūsoku kojitsu|ja|有職故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku kojitsu|1=Yūsoku Kojitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1623327}} studies on court customs during the {{ill|Insei period|ja|院政期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Insei period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 院政|1=Cloistered rule|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q865416}} through the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, resulting in the compilation of numerous ritual manuals ({{ill|Gishikisho|ja|儀式書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gishikisho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 儀式 (書物)|1=gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11387634}}). These texts reflected a reverence for and nostalgia toward past traditions.

== Establishment of Chōgi ==

The various court rituals of Chōgi each developed under different circumstances and at different times. However, they are closely linked to the adoption of {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|qid=Q4380197|lt=Tang law|1=Traditional Chinese law}}, and it is believed that a formalized national ceremonial structure was first established with the completion of the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|qid=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} in the first year of the {{ill|Taihō (era)|en|Taihō (era)|qid=Q1346269|lt=Taihō era|1=Taihō (era)}} (701 CE).

== See Also ==

* {{ill|Chōshū-den|ja|朝集殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōshū-den|1=Chōshūden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11517748}}
* {{ill|Imperial ceremonies|ja|皇室の儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial ceremonies|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Ritual Ceremonies of the Imperial Family|qid=Q11581030}}

* {{ill|Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|qq=}}
* {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese governmental systems|qq=}}
* {{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|en|Zenken Kojitsu|qid=Q11397827|lt=Zenken Kojitsu|1=Zenken Kojitsu}}
* {{ill|Historical verification|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical verification|qq=}}

== References ==

{{Reflist}}

== Sources ==

* Kurosu, Toshio (黒須利夫). "Chōsei to Chōgi" (朝政と朝儀). In *Nihon Kodai-shi Kenkyū Jiten* (日本古代史研究事典), edited by Abe Takeshi, Yoshie Akiko, Maki Michio, and Aiso Takashi. Tōkyōdō Shuppan, May 1995. ISBN 4-490-10396-4.

*
